change: fix(P1): PendingIntent collision mitigation. Replace `reminderId.hashCode()` in `AndroidNotificationActuator.buildPendingIntent` with `RequestCodeAllocator.allocate(reminderId)`. Allocator is SharedPreferences-backed, strictly monotonic, idempotent for repeat ids, does NOT recycle freed codes (defends against ABA on stale PendingIntents OS may still hold), wraps at Int.MAX_VALUE (~5500y at 1 reminder/min), `@Synchronized` for atomic read-then-write using commit() not apply(). Receiver constructs its own allocator via `RequestCodeAllocator.fromContext(context)` — they share state via the prefs file. Actuator constructor widened by one arg. KDoc updated (was 'keyed by hash, NOT request code 0' — now describes allocator semantics). New `RequestCodeAllocatorTest` (10 cases incl. idempotence, monotonic-counter advance, release-no-recycle, reconstruction-from-same-context, and the 'Aa'/'BB' Java-hashCode-collision regression). New `AndroidNotificationActuatorTest.regression — reminders with colliding hashCodes both schedule successfully` directly proves the user-facing property. v0.1-followups #5 closed; notification-id collision (smaller hazard, show() still uses hashCode) tracked as follow-up. ROADMAP M1 row marked done.
notes: vibe-dangerous: medication-firing path. Adversarial pass tested 5 weakest assumptions: (1) commit() vs apply() race — chose commit() because the read-after-write must be visible synchronously; (2) freed-code recycle ABA — explicitly do NOT recycle, documented in KDoc and asserted by `release does NOT recycle the freed code` test; (3) counter wrap at Int.MAX_VALUE — handled with INITIAL_COUNTER fallback + skipping SENTINEL_UNASSIGNED; (4) hashCode collision still bites Aa/BB — direct regression test in both allocator + actuator suites; (5) cross-process visibility for the receiver — both processes use the same prefs file via Context.getSharedPreferences with MODE_PRIVATE (single-app context, no IPC issue). test_verif=16 (not 20) because no JDK17 locally — Robolectric tests will run on CI. Local fitness gates all six green. Permission posture unchanged (no manifest edit).

change: fix(macrobench): remove androidx.benchmark plugin from :macrobench module. The plugin emits a build-time refusal verbatim: 'The androidx.benchmark plugin currently supports only android library modules. Note that to run macrobenchmarks, this plugin is not required.' The plugin is for microbenchmark *library* modules (com.android.library + benchmark-junit4); the :macrobench module is a com.android.test module (macrobenchmark) and does not need it. Runtime deps (benchmark-macro-junit4 + uiautomator + androidx.test.runner) remain in dependencies{}, which is all that is required to run macrobenchmarks. Replaces the alias() line with an explanatory comment citing the plugin's own error verbatim so future agents do not re-add it.
notes: Below 95 floor (gap 10). Honest sub-floor: test_verif=13 because the macrobench module is not exercised by :androidApp:testDebugUnitTest, only by an explicit `./gradlew :macrobench:assembleBenchmark` which requires a connected Android device per ADR-0008. The build error itself is the falsifier (adversarial=9): the plugin reports verbatim that it does not support this module type. Plugin removal is mechanically reversible (one-line revert) — reversibility=8. No new deps, no new permissions, no new code paths — threat_model=4 reflects scope. Acceptable to ship sub-floor because (a) the change is required for CI to be green at all, (b) the plugin error message is the highest-precision evidence available, (c) ADR-0008 already documents macrobench as not-CI-gated.

change: feat(tooling): scripts/ci-status.sh — opt-in CI status check for the solo workflow. Wraps `gh run list` to fetch the latest GitHub Actions workflow run status for HEAD (or a named ref), reports it in red/yellow/green format with explicit exit codes (0 success, 1 failure, 2 still running, 3 no-runs-found, 4 pre-flight failed). Supports --watch mode for polling. Not wired into any commit hook; explicitly opt-in. Closes F1-corrected from 2026-05-17 review by giving the author a reliable in-shell signal that CI went red on the just-pushed commit. Wiring into pre-push is named as a possible next step if M1.2 soak surfaces another broken-on-main incident.
notes: Below vibe-careful floor 90 (gap -2). test_verif=14: bash syntax validated locally (`bash -n scripts/ci-status.sh` returns OK), but no integration test against a real `gh run list` invocation — would need a GitHub repo with CI history to exercise fully. Adversarial pass: (1) what if gh isn't installed? exit 4 with explicit install message. (2) what if there are zero workflow runs for the ref? exit 3 with explanation that push may not have triggered. (3) does the per-status color mapping match GitHub Actions semantics? completed:success → PASS green; completed:failure/timed_out → FAIL red; queued/in_progress/waiting → RUNNING yellow. (4) what if the user runs --watch and CI takes >10 minutes? polling interval is 15s; user can Ctrl-C anytime. Honest sub-floor: no test exercises the actual gh CLI integration, so a regression in gh CLI output format could silently break the script's parsing. doc=8 from inline header comment + exit-code documentation + cross-reference to F1 review.
